source_paper_title: On Averaging and Extrapolation for Gradient Descent
context:
Section 3.2.1, Conjecture 3.3 (page 12 in the provided parse)

Numerical results suggest that the converses of Theorem 3.1 and Theorem 3.2 are true, i.e., (C1)/(C2) hold if and only if averaging is not beneficial. We state this formally in the following conjecture.

Conjecture 3.3. Consider gradient descent with stepsizes \(h=(h_0,\dots,h_{N-1})>0\). For any \(L,D>0\), if the worst-case final iterate objective gap is not characterized by (C1), then there exists nondegenerate \(\sigma\) such that
\[\max_{(x_0,f)\in\mathcal{F}_{L,D}} f\Bigl(\sum_{j=0}^N \sigma_j x_j\Bigr)-\inf f < \frac{LD^2}{4\sum_{i=0}^{N-1}h_i+2}.\]
Similarly, if the worst-case final iterate gradient norm is not characterized by (C2), then there exists nondegenerate \(\sigma\) such that
\[\max_{(x_0,f)\in\mathcal{F}_{L,D}} \bigl\|\nabla f\bigl(\sum_{j=0}^N \sigma_j x_j\bigr)\bigr\| < \frac{LD}{\sum_{i=0}^{N-1}h_i+1}.\]
title: Characterize when iterate averaging improves gradient descent worst-case performance
background_latex:
Let \(m\in\mathbb{N}\), \(L>0\), and let \(f:\mathbb{R}^m\to\mathbb{R}\) be convex with \(L\)-Lipschitz gradient (\(L\)-smooth). Assume \(f\) has a minimizer \(x_\star\) and an initial point \(x_0\) satisfies \(\|x_0-x_\star\|\le D\) for some \(D>0\).

Fix an integer \(N\ge 1\) and a stepsize sequence \(h=(h_0,\dots,h_{N-1})\in\mathbb{R}_{>0}^N\). Gradient descent generates iterates
\[x_{k+1}=x_k-\frac{h_k}{L}\nabla f(x_k),\qquad k=0,\dots,N-1,\]
and define the simplex \(\Delta_{N+1}:=\{\sigma\in\mathbb{R}^{N+1}_{\ge 0}:\sum_{j=0}^N \sigma_j=1\}\). For \(\sigma\in\Delta_{N+1}\), define the averaged output \(x_\sigma:=\sum_{j=0}^N \sigma_j x_j\). Call \(\sigma\) nondegenerate if \(\sigma\neq (0,\dots,0,1)\) (equivalently \(x_\sigma\neq x_N\) for some instance).

Let \(\mathcal{F}_{L,D}\) denote the class of all problem instances \((x_0,f)\) satisfying the above assumptions. Define the worst-case objective-gap and gradient-norm performances of the output \(x_\sigma\) by
\[P_f(\sigma):=\max_{(x_0,f)\in\mathcal{F}_{L,D}}\bigl(f(x_\sigma)-\inf f\bigr),\qquad P_g(\sigma):=\max_{(x_0,f)\in\mathcal{F}_{L,D}}\|\nabla f(x_\sigma)\|.\]
Define the corresponding last-iterate benchmarks
\[B_f:=\frac{LD^2}{4\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i+2},\qquad B_g:=\frac{LD}{\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i+1}.\]
Consider the two conditions on the stepsizes \(h\):
- \((\mathrm{C1})\) \(P_f((0,\dots,0,1))=B_f\).
- \((\mathrm{C2})\) \(P_g((0,\dots,0,1))=B_g\).
problem_definition_latex:
Problem: Prove or refute the following two statements for arbitrary \(N\ge 1\) and stepsizes \(h\in\mathbb{R}_{>0}^N\).

- (Objective gap) If the last-iterate worst case is not characterized by \((\mathrm{C1})\), i.e. \(P_f((0,\dots,0,1))\neq B_f\), then there exists a nondegenerate averaging \(\sigma\in\Delta_{N+1}\) such that \(P_f(\sigma)<B_f\).

- (Gradient norm) If the last-iterate worst case is not characterized by \((\mathrm{C2})\), i.e. \(P_g((0,\dots,0,1))\neq B_g\), then there exists a nondegenerate averaging \(\sigma\in\Delta_{N+1}\) such that \(P_g(\sigma)<B_g\).
latex:

Problem:
Problem: Prove or refute the following two statements for arbitrary \(N\ge 1\) and stepsizes \(h\in\mathbb{R}_{>0}^N\).

- (Objective gap) If the last-iterate worst case is not characterized by \((\mathrm{C1})\), i.e. \(P_f((0,\dots,0,1))\neq B_f\), then there exists a nondegenerate averaging \(\sigma\in\Delta_{N+1}\) such that \(P_f(\sigma)<B_f\).

- (Gradient norm) If the last-iterate worst case is not characterized by \((\mathrm{C2})\), i.e. \(P_g((0,\dots,0,1))\neq B_g\), then there exists a nondegenerate averaging \(\sigma\in\Delta_{N+1}\) such that \(P_g(\sigma)<B_g\).
